candidate_problems.text: Can a coherent, non-question-begging model of agent-causation be formulated that makes persons genuine causal primitives while still explaining how a single bodily action occurs under identical prior physical conditions? This is the central unresolved tension because the conjecture infers the need for agent-causation but does not specify its ontology, laws, or mechanism of intervention.

Is causal closure of physics actually the right target, or can free will be reconciled with physics through a non-reductive but physically consistent account of higher-level causation, where agents are real explanatory causes without violating lower-level lawfulness? This matters because the conjecture may assume too quickly that irreducible agency must mean incompleteness or breach of physics rather than lawful multilevel causation.

What principled criterion distinguishes agential branching from mere indeterministic noise, such that an undetermined outcome can be attributable to the person rather than to chance? This is an open problem because the conjecture correctly rejects randomness as sufficient for freedom, but leaves unspecified what additional structure turns indeterminism into authorship.
